extracts.extract: When considering undercover or surveillance work, journalists should ask: Is it possible to get convincing evidence through documents and interviews instead? What significant points will undercover work or surveillance add to the story in terms of accuracy and credibility? Will the results of the story be important and alarming enough to overshadow the deceptive practices?

The attempts to contact a person should be thoroughly documented. A letter can be sent by registered mail, with a return receipt requested. An email can be sent with preferences set to see if the recipient has opened it. Direct messages can be sent via social media. Going to a person's office or home and keeping careful records on the time and date also help show that every effort was made to reach him or her.

Journalists can take three steps to lessen the controversy of using anonymous sources: First, try hard to obtain the information from on-the-record human sources and from documents. Second, try hard to persuade the original off-the-record source to go public. Third, when nothing else works, explain fully why the source has requested and been granted anonymity.

Since newsrooms can now be bypassed by leakers on the internet, the investigative reporter is not an arbiter of what should be made public, but a journalist who provides accuracy, context and standards of fairness is ensuring as little harm is done as possible.

The reporter goes back to the original documentation to check out each fact and that it is supported by documents and/or interviews. The reporter checks quotes from the story with transcribed interviews to ascertain accuracy and context. The line-by-line procedure is also meant to spot logical inconsistencies or information gaps.
When doing line-by-line checking, it is important to check individual facts like names, titles and addresses against third-party sources, not against a reporter's own notes.

Good reporters highlight every single fact in a story — names, numbers, quotes — and meticulously check each. Check the spelling of every name against a reliable source such as the subject's social media site or biography page on their workplace. For claims like 'terrified,' find the origin of the specific wording — was it described in a police report or from a direct quote? If you did the analysis, redo the data work to make sure you get the same result. See if anyone else has done the same analysis and gotten similar results.
A simple rule applies to everything on social media, the same way it does to any other potential fact a reporter uncovers — if you can't verify it, you can't use it.

Many journalists read quotes or passages back to sources or show them paragraphs on complex or technical topics. Steve Weinberg makes a strong argument for allowing subjects to review the text of stories, to ensure accuracy and to avoid surprises upon publication. The idea of fact-checking quotes and context by reading sections of a story has been practiced by many publications.

As more information has gone digital, databases or sets of documents have become a key component of collaborations. Documents or data that can be sliced into subsets by geography or topic allow journalists from different regions and newsrooms to work from the same baseline.

Before deciding to collaborate, journalists should: Decide whether the collaboration is truly needed. Decide who will lead and coordinate the effort. Be clear on what each partner can and will bring to the story, whether it is editorial content, data analysis, a particular media format or a distribution method. Identify the strengths and weaknesses of each partner at the beginning. Set up a routine of weekly communication. Determine the security level needed to protect communication. Have a graceful exit strategy including a written memo of understanding.

At their core, collaborations are about building trust. Reporters trained to compete fiercely for every scrap of news have a natural suspicion of outsiders. Initial phases are crucial in making a collaboration work. If possible, the first meeting to discuss a joint project should take place in person. Conference calls are a lousy way to launch a relationship.

ICIJ thoroughly examines a reporter's track record before inviting them to collaborate. Among the questions: What work has the reporter produced? What has been written about the journalist's work? What do colleagues say about the reporter's journalism and ethics? Has the journalist's work been recognized in articles or awards?

As we coordinate requests for comment we make sure that everyone understands that giving enough time for people to answer questions is crucial. ICIJ provides hands-on training on journalism techniques during the investigations and infuses those sessions with standards. In sessions like those, reporters have asked our views about doing hidden cameras. We have helped them find other ways to report the story every time possible.
The solution to the collaboration with WikiLeaks proved to be similar to how journalists have dealt with leakers and whistleblowers: Verify the leaked documents and data.

The team also included the University of Oregon's Agora Journalism Center, tapping into its expertise in engagement to help involve members of the local community in the project, particularly high school teachers, students and parents. The Center also had training for young reporters on how to collect and analyze the data and included a volunteer researcher from Portland State University to review statistical conclusions of the stories.

Crowdsourcing options include: Reporting individual tips as stand-alone stories, particularly useful when working with local media outlets. Identifying patterns in the data and reporting as many tips as possible that are part of that pattern. Using the database that is compiled to identify sources for stories you're already working on.
In most cases, the investigative journalist is measuring the employees' or agency's performance against well-articulated and understood standards, regulations and laws.

Before checking out a tip or planning a methodical investigation into a city or agency, an investigative journalist can begin collecting basic information: the list of all employees with name, department, title, date of hire, salary and date of birth; all those appointed to commissions and boards; all vendors who supply materials and how much they receive; campaign finance reports from the last four years; the list of grants given out; statements of potential conflicts of interest; and the last three years of budget and audits.

A budget is a key document for looking into waste, fraud and abuse. Budgets show where the money comes from (revenue) and where the money goes (expenditures), and they also reveal what and who are important to the government. One useful maxim to remember is 'Budget is policy, policy is budget.'

Budgets can be misleading because some expenditures may be contained in another government program that appears unrelated, or in the federal government it may be 'off-budget.' Some governments do not make clear the future pension liabilities in current budgets, even though the money will have to be paid out. That means large obligations to spend money will drive up deficits and go unnoticed by citizens and most journalists.
Don't wait for the new budget to come out. Get started now with the current budget so you'll become familiar with it and be ready to address any changes from it to the new budget.
extracts.notes: Walter Dawson and Jimmie Covington, Memphis Commercial Appeal

extracts.extract: If we only look at pluses and minuses compared to last year, we are accepting on faith the other, larger part of the budget. Our attention might be directed to a police chief's request for ten additional detectives, but not to the uses of the 900 officers already on duty. Like an iceberg, the largest part of the budget escapes our gaze.
extracts.notes: Frederick O'Reilly Hayes via Paul Williams

extracts.extract: The best tactic for a journalist unfamiliar with contracting is to look at a well-defined government task and examine it step-by-step. The journalist should begin with the enabling law and the executive-branch regulation implementing the law.

A journalist should obtain the cost estimate and then determine whether the awarded contract was below, at or above the estimate. If there is a significant discrepancy, the journalist ought to figure out why. A review of small purchases during previous years might reveal a deliberate plan to shift nonbid business to a political contributor, a friend or a company in which the government administrator or a relative has a financial interest.

An investigative journalist can check each company that bids or wins a bid and ask: Does it owe back taxes? Do its officers, directors, stockholders, lawyers, accountants or other key players have links to members of the government awarding the contract? Has the company failed to finish jobs satisfactorily in the past? Are there post-contract audits that document past problems? Have any lawsuits resulted from the company's alleged poor performance?

Investigative journalists should determine whether specifications favor a particular supplier by purposefully excluding all but one brand of equipment. Narrowly drawn specifications ought to be a red flag. Journalists also can check to see if the potential supplier helped the agency write the terms or if the supplier is a political contributor.

While change orders can be justified, it is one way for a contractor that made the lowest bid to earn more money. The potential for dishonesty is especially high with change orders because they are not subjected to competitive bidding. Even when the change orders are for relatively small dollar amounts, they can add up quickly as large cost overruns and a significant percentage of the total contract.

Audits generally contain two sections: the management advisory report and the financial statement. The management advisory report shows compliance or noncompliance with laws and regulations, deviations from sound business practices and the program's degree of effectiveness. There are four kinds of audit opinions: unqualified (clean), qualified (with weaknesses), adverse (serious/criminal problems), and disclaimer of opinion (insufficient records).

Since so much federal money goes to states and local governments, inspectors general reports can be valuable tip sheets to local scandals in federal programs. Journalists should also look for wrongdoing more urgent than what is mentioned in the semiannual report; in cases of particularly serious or flagrant problems, inspectors general are supposed to contact the relevant agency head immediately.

Investigative journalists can begin a corruption check by examining financial disclosures given by executive-branch supervisors. A savvy investigative journalist will also examine disclosures in search of corporate directorships. When an agency official is a bank director, it is legitimate to ask whether the bank has interests pending before the agency.

One common conflict is the revolving door between government positions and those in the private sector. These are difficult to monitor because the comings and goings are frequent and intentionally low-profile. Title 10, Section 2397, of the U.S. Code requires Defense Department military and civilian employees above certain levels to file a report if hired by a major defense contractor.

The Federal Register is a tool for investigative journalists interested in monitoring the executive branch. A particularly valuable issue is the semiannual agenda of regulations published by each agency, listing contact persons, addresses, emails and direct telephone numbers. The agenda items are organized by program office and divided by stage of development: pre-rulemaking actions, proposed rules, recent final rules and recent completed actions.

These stories are initiated by a close scrutiny of public documents, such as legal ads for state bids in newspapers, state vendor records, civil lawsuits, conflict-of-interest statements by state officials, governor's calendars and both political campaign contributions and expenditures. Journalists should also review expenditures in the governor's office and track campaign contributions and lobbyist spending and compare those documents to political appointments, legislation and the awarding of contracts.

Following the money from outside interests to legislators is a good way to start an investigation. Campaign contribution and expenditure reports are more accessible than ever before, and on the federal and often the state level, there are databases that can be quickly searched, sorted and grouped for that information.

The best stories go beyond raw dollars to look at why an individual or institution donates and what they get in return. Whenever a candidate receives a contribution from outside the district, journalists should ask why a nonconstituent cares enough to give. If individuals and corporations within the same industry are donating to the same member of Congress, ask: What does the industry do? Do the companies often receive government contracts, grants or subsidies? Is there legislation the industry is pushing that could increase its profits?

Think of Dark Money research as a fractal equation. Your original, core set of information and the tools you know you have at your disposal are the equation. Each time you run the equation you are gathering a few more names, a few more addresses, and a few more vendors or associated groups, then you plug all of those new pieces of information back into the equation, and gradually widen the network of stakeholders relevant to your research.
extracts.notes: Robert Maguire, Center for Responsive Politics

extracts.extract: Gathering names and addresses on the forms used to create a nonprofit and then comparing those listings with state lobbyist registries, incorporation documents kept by secretary of state offices and other publicly available documents can help connect the dots.

We were able to ascertain the top ten U.S. officials most frequently contacted by Mexican officials or their paid representatives by breaking FARA documents down into tens of thousands of facts — names, numbers, contacts made — and entering them into a database.
extracts.notes: Charles Lewis, Center for Public Integrity

extracts.extract: At all levels of government, journalists should push legislators to go beyond the limited disclosure in the forms by supplying federal and state income tax returns. An obvious question is whether a legislator's lifestyle appears to surpass what his or her stated income suggests is reasonable. Simply comparing a representative's latest financial disclosures with his or her first one as a member of Congress can be enlightening.

It is also worth looking at where legislation comes from. In many cases, the lawmaker who sponsors a bill had little or nothing to do with drafting it. Special-interest groups, think tanks and other interested parties often draft 'model legislation,' and then look for willing lawmakers to carry it. The USA Today network did a major investigation of model legislation using a computer algorithm and found more than 10,000 bills in an eight-year period that were taken from model legislation.

Although GAO reports are treated by many daily journalists as self-contained stories, they should be a starting point. One value of GAO reports is the description of the agency or program goals and the specificity of recommendations for reform. A journalist delving into an issue can report on failures to comply with GAO recommendations.

Hearings records can be unparalleled resources for information gatherers. But proceed carefully — sometimes the hearing is stacked to reflect the views of the chair. A journalist should check whether the witness list is weighted toward one side and whether relevant industry interests are contributors to committee lawmakers.

The justice system has clear junctures where documents are produced, decisions are made, and the roles of individuals are sharply defined. At each one of these junctures, there are numerous investigative possibilities. Court files provide documents and data that can be used in stories that go far beyond the judicial system.
Any reporter studying an individual or institution should not only check the clips but always check state and federal court cases for background and story leads.

By talking to lawyers, law professors, clerks and fellow judges, an investigative journalist can gauge whether a judge is considered to favor plaintiffs or defendants. Journalists should know which judges issue more contempt citations than average and then inquire why. Examining motions in court proceedings can reveal refusals to step aside from cases with conflicts of interest.

One measure of a prosecutor's performance is how many cases go to trial. If almost every case goes to trial, something is amiss because some cases brought by police are flawed and ought to be dismissed. If almost no cases go to trial, too many defendants may be getting off too lightly with plea bargains. Journalists should also look for cases that end in convictions on something less than the original charge, which is called undercharging.

Civil case files can be obtained from the court clerk, the lawyers, the plaintiffs or the defendants. The docket sheet is the best place to begin — it lists all filings by both parties and all actions of the court in chronological order. Discovery materials can include office memoranda and depositions, which are sworn oral statements taken from a witness before trial.

A criminal case can be a long and complicated trail of events and documents, but attention to detail can make all the difference. Each step in the case produces motions and records that deserve close scrutiny and often reveal injustices when they occur.
extracts.chapter: Tracking the Criminal Case: From Arrest to Sentencing

extracts.extract: Philadelphia Inquirer reporters studied transcripts of pretrial hearings from homicide cases and were able to document a pattern of police beating confessions out of suspects. The Inquirer found 80 cases during a three-year period in which judges refused to allow confessions into evidence.

When the system allows judge shopping, a journalist can determine whether certain lawyers appear before certain judges more often than chance would suggest. The journalist can look at which lawyers win rulings or cases often before particular judges and determine whether there are business connections or social ties between judge and lawyer.

The Florida Times-Union determined that defendants who killed white victims received much longer prison sentences than defendants who killed black victims. For federal crimes, the U.S. Sentencing Commission instructs judges on how to consider each convict's criminal history. When measuring effectiveness, journalists should compare the sentencing patterns of individual judges with those of the entire court. Many courts now have databases that track each case so a journalist can more easily look for patterns.

Bankruptcies filed by individuals or corporations provide detailed information about the party in question. From the records at the court, journalists might get the subject's Social Security number, bank account balances, landholdings, personal property, stocks, bonds and income. Bankruptcy forms also can contain information about gambling debts.

NJ Advance Media created a statewide database covering five years and more than 70,000 use-of-force incidents. Among their findings: Black people were three times as likely to face use of force from police, and only 10% of officers accounted for almost 40% of all use-of-force cases.

Journalists should monitor police officers from recruitment to retirement using these techniques: Observing officers directly. Studying incident and arrest records and rates. Tracking day-to-day prevention efforts. Talking regularly to citizens in neighborhoods. Knowing the leaders of the police union. Studying budgets and expenditures. Reading personnel files when available. Checking for Internal Affairs investigations. Following lawsuits in local courts. Checking accreditation status.

Reporters should check screening protocols at the local police department. Are résumés checked carefully? Understanding how thorough the backgrounding process is can be crucial in understanding how bad hires are made.

Sandra Peddie recommends several places to look when police disciplinary documents are not available: Lawsuits — read the complaints and all related documents, and ask attorneys for transcripts of depositions. Incident reports — request the entire report, including the narrative, not just the one-page summary. Police handbooks — these include rules and regulations and provide a way to determine if actions violate policies.

Journalists should ask whether local police departments have instituted a policy of video recording interrogations to minimize mistreatment. When recordings are made, journalists should seek to view them regularly, just as they make a regular practice of seeking and reading arrest reports. Another way of tracking problem officers is to examine transfers within departments to lower-profile or less desirable assignments.

Journalists should also scrutinize agencies for what they sell as well as what they buy. Government agencies regularly dispose of surplus equipment at prices that may benefit purchasers rather than the general tax-paying public.

A journalist should also check whether incumbents continue to receive income from previous jobs and how many hours they spend at that job. The time taken could affect performance in office, or it could be that the job is a fiction, providing the employer with access to an influential legislator. A journalist should not stop with the legislator but should find out whether friends and relatives have been benefiting from legislative decisions.

A journalist should check every line on the politician's résumé, looking for exaggerations or outright fabrications. The journalist can examine the candidate's reputation in past jobs and simply do online searches or talk to employees, employers, family members, neighbors, school classmates and teachers to piece together their assessment — the usual range of 'currents' and 'formers.'

In the permanent bureaucracy, a journalist should cultivate low-level employees on the organization charts, such as clerks and custodial workers. They hear and see much, can supply copies of documents if they want to and can sometimes have little loyalty because of their low pay and status.

The Advocate in Baton Rouge determined that two-thirds of the contributors to the incumbent mayor were doing direct business with the city government. The newspaper's investigative journalists made the determination by matching campaign contributions with payout vouchers.

If journalists cannot do the risky work of going undercover, they can examine prison complaint files, interview recently released prisoners and check lawsuits filed by prisoners in federal and state courts. Journalists also can contact former employees, defense lawyers and the family members of prisoners to increase their knowledge of prison conditions.
